turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Number oh oh five oh seven, the Chickasaw Nation versus the United States. Mr. Luthey. [Inaudible]

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. The Chickasaw and Choctaw Nations ask this Court to hold that Congress has afforded tribes the same exemption from Federal wagering taxes enjoyed by State governmental wagering operations. We do not base our claim on policy untethered to statutory text, but instead we bla- base our claim today on section twenty D of the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act codified at twenty-five USC, section twenty-seven nineteen D. Unfortunately, for all concerned, that statutory text is ambiguous, as every appellate judge which has examined it has held either expressly or implicitly, and as we expect the Government to admit today if the question is put to them. One of the possible constructions of that ambiguous statute, based on its text and structure and giving effect to every word of the statute and bolstered by its legislative history, is that the tax exemption afforded States by Internal Revenue Code chapter thirty-five, expressly referenced in section twenty-seven nineteen D, applies equally to tribal wagering operations. [Inaudible]

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Where do we find this exact text that you're talking about in the brief or in...

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I would direct Your Honor to the petitioner's appendix ninety A,

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: to our appendix for certiorari. Well, haven't you set it out on...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: page three?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: On page three of your brief? That's a handier reference I think if,

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Now starting with the actual text of the statute, we believe that the statute contains two central textual commands, one that has to do with the reporting and withholding of taxes from gaming, from wei- from the winnings from gaming, and also the application of the Internal Revenue Code provisions concerning wagering operations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to apply...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: before you proceed further with this argument that you make in your reply brief, did you present it, this or distinguishing gaming on the one hand from wagering operations? Did you present that to the Tenth Circuit?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Not directly like that, Your Honor, we did not.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And did you present it in your opening brief?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: What we did, Your Honor, in the opening brief is adopt the decision by the majority in the Little Six decision from the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Federal circuit.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: make this argument.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: No.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, you've answered the question.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: To address specifically, Your Honor, the Government's position which we claim is based on a false premise.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That was not a new position.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: The way we would have the provision read, Your Honor, is set out at page nine.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Page what?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Page nine of the reply brief where we suggest that concerning addresses two distinct central textual commands...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: in in...

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: in contradiction, Your Honor, to the Government's assertion that there is one central...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: textual command.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: and you would say that the way to read it is, concerning the re- t- the way it reads, if one would read it normally, is, concerning the reporting and withholding of taxes with respect to the winnings from gaming or wagering operations.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Your Honor, if I could...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: respond.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I wish you would.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I th- I think our reading is informed by the parenthetical reference that precedes these two concepts, and particularly the text and structure of that parenthetical reference.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that. The govern-

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: the winnings from wagering.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: From gaming.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But not from wagering.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Yeah, it's from gaming by players, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: of the parenthetical...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I thought that gaming, a gaming operations or wagering operations, perhaps you can clarify what is the difference between gaming as a noun and wagering operations?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I- It's our, a- and you hit hit right exactly on the point, Your Honor.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But not the winnings by players from wagering.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And and what...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: what is the difference between those two?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I- the the difference is how it is used in the context of this statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: parenthetical.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: tell me concretely what is the difference between winnings from gaming and winnings from wagering operations.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Winnings from gaming here is refers to winnings by the players from gaming, money paid to players as a result of gaming, winnings from what...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the players do.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Is is there a difference, just in the English language, between gaming and wagering?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Th- they they could be regarded synonymous if both are used as nouns, Mr. Chief Justice, or both are used as adjectives.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: two...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: ask? Th- the- under your reading, what, using Justice Scalia's A and B, what function does A play?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: What function A plays is that the tax is imposed on the winnings from gaming as a result of what the players have won.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Wouldn't that have been picked up anyway, just in your reading of from wagering on?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Not necessarily...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: b-

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: because when you get to the second set, Your Honor, particularly sixty fifty I and chapter thirty-five, the text of those provisions have nothing to do with winnings.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: No.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Well...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So, wouldn't that have been covered just as easily by simply saying concerning wagering operations?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Well, section fourteen forty-one and thirty-four oh two actually do impose a tax, a percentage of tax, that is on the winnings from gaming.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: That that's the chapter we're okay,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The chapter thirty-five...

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: i- is different, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: a cha- a tax on the operator based on the wagers that have been received, not the money that the operator has paid out.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Let me put it this way, and it's suggested by Justice Ginsburg's line of questioning.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: It is the only reason it makes sense.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, then that, it seems to me, destroys your argument because you you you you are, you want to say that gaming covers the entire universe of winnings that are subject to withholding.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I- If you would...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So, it seems to me that...

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: des- that destroys the distinction you're trying to make which is what Justice Ginsburg's line of questioning, it seemed, suggested to me at least.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Wi- With respect, Your Honor, if you look at wagering and gaming as nouns, they could be read to refer to the same thing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It has nothing...

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: to do with it.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: The oddity of the argument to me is that the, you're assum- I think your explanation is assuming that the basic activity that's going on in a casino, which is giving rise to each of these different kinds of liability, is the same activity.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: I guess you're calling it wagering operations, not wagering.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: right?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: that's that's exactly correct,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: the operation, th- the wagering operation for one purpose is gaming for another purpose, and that, and in your, as I understand your argument, and that's just a very strange and confusing usage to impute to the Congress.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: W Wi- With respect, Your Honor, if i- if the statute is read closely, the first three provisions of the parenthetical apply to concerning the reporting and withholding of taxes with respect to the winnings.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: winnings. They come from gaming.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Then we have two additional provisions identified, sixty fifty I of the Internal Revenue Code and chapter thirty-five...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: And that's reporting and withholding, and the activity of the reporting and withholding is not gaming, but wagering operation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No, b- well...

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I I I I'm I'm not being clear here.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Nothing to do with reporting and withholding.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: consistently, and the Government will concede that.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: don't they impose an excise tax on operators?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: They do.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Gaming and wagering? If you if you have a casino, you have to pay an excise...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct. Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: And you're arguing that the t- Indian tribes that operate the casinos were given somehow the same exemption from that that States are when States operate lotteries or some kind of gambling operation.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Other type of wagering operation,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Your Honor. That...

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well before you get to that, I can take a break when you get to that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Do any State constitutions allow...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the State to, , you know, to gamble with the with the public funds? Y- See what I'm saying? At the end of it, State, you can't possibly read it as, apply to State gaming on the one hand and wagering operations on the other because States don't game.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: A- At the end of, at the end of the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: statute, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: one way at the end and another way in the middle.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Well, when you say gaming at the middle, don't you...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: mean gaming...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: by a player in a casino?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Okay, and isn't that what you would also mean when the word gaming is used at the end?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Well, th-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: this is...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: buy your argument, but I'm not sure that I see the inconsistency.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: If you're going to go to legislative history, why doesn't it all become clear? Originally there was a bill that had the word not only the reporting but also taxations.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I'd l- I'd like, Your Honor, to offer a third explanation in response to your direct question.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: That Congress ended.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But, counsel...

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: explanation, but the third explanation then depends on the testimony of the Indian tribal interests that say they don't want the word taxation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: but I haven't read it that...

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: you will find that in the amicus brief filed by Little Six at page twenty-five.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Counsel, it seems to me you have two different kinds of presumptions that we face w- with language that can be said to be ambiguous.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: In Mescalero, Your Honor, there were actually two particular taxes at issue.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: There's a well recognized presumption, Mr. Luthey.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: The underlying trust relationship, Your Honor, between the United States and the Indians, its wards, as recognized by this Court in the County of Oneida...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: decision.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: but y- y- y- w- wh- g- give me a better reason than that.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Because the statute is imposed by Congress on its wards, and this Court has long recognized, including in the Montana versus Blackfeet Tribe, that statutes concerning Indians that have ambiguity are to be construed in favor of the Indians.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Mr. Luthey, no no...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you had your finger on your button before I did.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: to that?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: direct Your Honor to Squire versus Capoeman at three fifty-one US one where this Court dealt with a Federal tax, a claimed exemption from Federal taxation,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah...

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: and applied the Indian canon in favor of the tax exemption sought by the Indians.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Mr. Luthey, may I...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: could I ask you? You You say they're just making the Indian tribes similar to the States.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I'm not aware of any at this time,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: So So the plausibility of of of of giving a an exemption from tax here to the t- t- to the Indian casinos, because after all the States have it, is really not all that plausible because I I don't know any State that has gone into the casino business, nor do I think any is about to.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: With respect, Your Honor, I I I must disagree with you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: D- Do some tr-

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: may I a- ask you one question on on the text that supports Justice Breyer's notion that maybe this was just a crazy drafter? The sta- section starts out, the provisions of the Internal Revenue Code of nineteen eighty-six.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I I would suggest to Your Honor that the provisions of the Internal Revenue Code of nineteen eighty-six are then explained after the parenthetical by specifically what the provisions concern that are to be applied in the same way as States, and specifically included in that is chapter thirty-five which contains the exemption for States.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: A- And in fact...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: the sentence then to start sections so and so and so and so concerning? Why have the provisions of the Internal Revenue Code?

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: I- The the the lead-in, the provisions of the Internal Revenue Code, is limited by its by the part of the statute that identifies what the internal revenue concerns that shall be applicable equally for State gaming, as well as for the tribal activity here.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Luthey.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: You must be excited about defending this statute.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I'm always excited to appear before you, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It makes no...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: reference to Indian wagering operations i- i- in chapter thirty-five? Just States?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Chapter thirty-five makes no...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: makes no reference...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Makes no reference,

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: and it does not, I should point out, exempt States from all taxes.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: W- J- Just Just not just lotteries.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I think actually that the exemption is more limited than that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: In chapter thirty-five?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Is that in the papers before us?

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: problem here? Aren't we talking about casinos here?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: If you look at page...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to lotteries, , gee, we don't have a problem.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: It's page ninety-two A of the petition appendix, reprints section forty-four oh two which ec- lists the exemptions.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And is that what they're relying on in this case, that that that that exemption as as exempting casinos?

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: That's the exemption, although the the gambling at issue here is so-called pull-tabs which are a form of lottery, not a form of table gaming.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: tax. If...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: pull-tab? I've never pulled a...

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: pull-tab is a little card about like this with three to five little windows, like an Advent calendar, that you can pull up and reveal something underneath them.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: What a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: weird analogy.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: And if you find under it three angels, for instance, you may you may win something.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Of course, in Calvinist...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: theology, it's not weird at all.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: So, in any event, we're not talking here about Indian casinos, gambling casino operations, because they wouldn't be covered by the exemption in Title thirty-five even if it applied.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: on how broadly one construes the word lottery, and that's been broadly construed in some cases.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: That's true.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: specific...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: this case we do have an Indian lottery.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: We have an Indian lottery.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: is are y- are we talking about, is is the excise tax of, point two five percent on the amount of each wager in forty-four oh one A one does that apply to Indian gaming? It says, there shall be imposed on any wager authorized under the law of the State in which accepted an a- an excise tax equal to zero point two five percent of the amount of such wager.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: That's the tax.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: wager.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: the tax we're...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That is the tax we're...

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: talking about.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: See, that's what I what I'm thinking of, is is the, what I'm thinking about is since forty-four A forty-four oh one A one, I E there shall be a quarter of one percent tax on every wager authorized under the law of the State, except later on, A, B, C, D.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: That's that's certainly correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Code. Now, that would not...

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: be a foregone conclusion to many tribal lawyer-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I can understand that.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: We do not have a good explanation for that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Good.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: And that was that you you subscribe to the position that Judge Dyke took in the Federal circuit because he said there wasn't a good explanation for it either.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: How about the reference to section sixty fifty I? Do you have an explanation for that?

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: It's a lot more understandable for the following reason.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Is there someone over in the IRS whose job it is to look through bills, when they finally emerge, and check the cross references and the numbers who might have caught what seems to be, in your argument certainly, an accidental error in not taking a number out that should have been taken out? Is there anyone there whose job it is to do that? I would think there was.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: There are...

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: a lot of people both at the IRS and on the Hill whose job it is to comb through tax legislation.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: After the split, though, between the Tenth Circuit and the Federal circuit, did anyone call the responsible committee's attention to this so that Congress could fix up the statute?

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I'm not aware that anyone has brought this issue to, onto the legislative agenda.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: in that...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: surprising in the broad sense that Congress clearly has favored Indian gaming operations and has wanted to provide income to the tribes for their purposes through those operations, and to impose an excise tax on it when the Congress doesn't impose it on the States would be perfectly consistent with that overall objective, it seems to me.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I think it would have been perfectly consistent to grant the exemption.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: sort of why...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: as consistent because the effort over there in Congress has been to support these Indian gaming operations, not that I necessarily think it's a good idea, but Congress obviously does.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: Well, but recall, Justice O'Connor, that in the specific context of the Indian Gaming Regulatory Act, w- what was going on was not a unilateral project in favor of tribal interests.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: on...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: is not, h- I mean, it you're you're saying that part of the compromise might be that the States end up without taxation and the Indians get taxed.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I think your competitive analogy exac- is exactly right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: a leftover or inadvertent reference to chapter thirty-five.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: something else? May may I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: go to. No, go ahead.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Before you depart from that subject, I I assume that there there there would be a a a mo- a motive for for the States to want to have these taxes imposed on the Indian lotteries even though they are not imposed on the State lotteries.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: It's a very small tax, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's a small tax.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: If it were a...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: bigger tax I think...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The odds are...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: pretty pretty close,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I mean, you know.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: May I now ask you to turn to to something else? I I asked counsel on the other side about the significance of your argument that the presumption favoring the the tribes in this situation should not operate, even though it might in a case of State taxation, because of the Government's plenary power over Indians as as distinct from the States' right only by sufferance of the Federal Government to tax.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I think because of the historical difference in the relations between the Federal Government and the tribes on the one hand and the State governments and the tribes on the other hand, and the best case I can point you to on that is the Kagama case from the end of the last century which talks about the trust relationship and it talks about the fact that the tribes stand in a very different relation to the Federal Government from the relation in which they stand to the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: States.

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: that that certainly affects, we'll assume the obligation of the Government, and it certainly may well accept the m- the specific decisions that the Government makes.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I think there are...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: two things that I'd l-

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: counterintuitive, I guess.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: Well, there are two things I'd like to point out about that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: revenue statutes in a way that...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: tell us that in- informs us in in construction? It it tells us something about the Government's obligations to various groups of people, but I don't know what it tells us about about meaning.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I'm not sure it tells you very much at all about meaning.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: construction for all the same reasons...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: I'm assuming you got two tie-breakers and they're at odds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: for all the same reasons...

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: that the trust relationship exists in the first place...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes, but you say...

turns.name: DAVID SOUTER
turns.text: they're a- they're at they're n- they're not or the the the the apparent loggerhead should should dissolve when you realize that the Government has a particular trust relationship to Indians.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I don't suggest that in in cases involving Federal taxation because there is an an equally strong and and, in this particular context, opposite principle that exemptions from the exactions, the general exactions, to support the Federal Government are to be narrowly construed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And more more to the point,

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: they're not to be emp-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: inferred.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: supposing we didn't have here an Indian tribe but an indi- individual Indian who was claiming exemption from taxation.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: Not in the same not in the same way.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: if I could, to the other reason...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: before we get off of canons, I w- we've just been talking about two, but there's a third one.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: T- To be sure,

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: the Indians' proposed interpretation here is strained, but you can do it where whereas there is no possible way to read section thirty-five on your interpretation as being relevant.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: ninety A of, yeah of the appendix...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to the petition.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: But page ninety A.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But...

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I don't think it's just a strained reading, I think it's an impossible reading...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But you do concede that, a- as I believe Judge Dyke said, the only way you can make sense out of the statute is to treat it as though the reference to chapter thirty-five were not there.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I think that's right.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Maybe I can just ask you one clarifying question.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Well, the one we're talking about in the case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: The whole this whole tax...

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: or the ta- or the wagering tax?

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: The the wagering tax.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: The wagering tax i- the definitions the n- the definitions on page ninety-seven A exempt from, they define define a l- a wager as sports wagering or forty-four twenty-one one C, any wager placed in a lottery conducted for profit.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: It does exclude...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: them.

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: excludes those.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: of the...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Of what?

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: Of the main gray brief, the gray brief on the merits.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That would have had to come up...

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: through the Ways and Means Committee if they had done that?

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: I suspect it would have had to go through the tax writing...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: committee certainly,

turns.name: EDWARD C. DUMONT
turns.text: yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. DuMont.

turns.name: GRAYDON D. LUTHEY, JR.
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Luthey.
